title: Church Government, Part 4: Elder and Related Terms in the New Testament; Plurality of Elders; Elders Are to Rule
speaker: Pastor Tito Lyro

outline.title: Review of the Series So Far
subpoints: God loves His church and has appointed a government for it
Authority is good (God's creation gift), can be misused (Satan's scheme), and is Christ's claim to redeem
Submission is the path to growth, authority, and likeness to Jesus; it is never absolute
Different models of church government were surveyed; Christ is the only head
Elder, bishop, and pastor are synonyms referring to the same office—reviewed from previous lessons

outline.title: Elder and Related Terms in the New Testament
subpoints: The Septuagint translates the Hebrew 'zaqen' (elder) with 'presbyteros'—the words have the same semantic range in both Testaments
'Presbyteros' is used 67 times in the New Testament; 60 uses relate to the office (not age); 7 to age
In the Gospels, 'elders of Israel' always refers to the rulers opposing Jesus; the first favorable use for Christian leaders is Acts 11:30
From Acts 11:30 onward, 'elder/presbyteros' is virtually always used for Christian church leaders
'Episcopos' (bishop/overseer) comes from the Greek world and is used interchangeably with 'presbyteros'; Christ Himself is called an 'episcopos' in 1 Peter 2:25

outline.title: The Scriptural Teaching of a Plurality of Elders
subpoints: The terms elder, bishop, and pastor already imply plurality when used of the church—they are almost always in the plural
Acts 14:23: Paul and Barnabas 'appointed elders in every church'—plural elders, singular every church
Acts 20:17: Paul called for 'the elders of the church' at Ephesus—a single church with multiple elders
Acts 20:28: 'The Holy Spirit has made you overseers'—plural overseers over one flock
James 5:14: 'Is anyone among you sick? Let him call for the elders of the church'—the earliest New Testament book, showing plurality from the very start of the apostolic church

outline.title: This Plurality Is Not Only Presbyterian
subpoints: Southern Baptist minister Mark Dever: 'The Bible clearly models a plurality of elders in each local church'
Martin Bucer (first-generation Reformer, continental Reformed tradition) held that the Holy Spirit 'wishes to have elders in each congregation'
Iain Murray (Reformed Church of Australia) notes the parallel with the Old Testament: each tribe and city had elders
Even non-Presbyterians who take the Bible seriously are increasingly recognizing the biblical pattern of plurality
Some Baptist/non-denominational churches now distinguish 'staff elders' from 'non-staff elders' to acknowledge this distinction

outline.title: Elders Are to Rule the Church
subpoints: The Bible does not speak of 'elder-led' churches as distinct from 'elder-ruled' churches; the distinction is a modern evangelical attempt to preserve congregationalism while acknowledging the biblical data
1 Timothy 5:17: 'Let the elders who rule well be counted worthy of double honor'—the word 'rule' (prohistemi) means to rule, not merely to lead
1 Thessalonians 5:12: 'Those who are over you in the Lord'—same word as in 1 Timothy 5:17
1 Timothy 3:4-5: An elder must be 'one who rules his own house well... for if a man does not know how to rule his own house, how will he take care of the church of God?'
Hebrews 13:17: 'Obey those who rule over you and be submissive'—the word is 'obey,' not 'decide whether to be led by them'

outline.title: The Jerusalem Council and the Authority of Elders
subpoints: The Jerusalem Council (Acts 15) expected all churches to obey its conclusion—this was not merely a suggestion
'It seemed good to the Holy Spirit and to us to lay upon you no greater burden than these necessary things'—a declaration of authoritative rule
The Form of Government of the BPC describes the session's power to inquire into faith and conduct, receive members, suspend or exclude from sacraments, and oversee all activities of the church
The elders have authority over every organization within the local church
The bumper sticker 'Question Authority' originated with Satan; questioning God's authority was the first sin

summary:
Pastor Tito Lyro presents lesson four of the Church Government series, focusing on the New Testament use of elder-related terminology and the positive scriptural teaching of a plurality of elders who are to rule each local congregation. The lesson begins with a comprehensive review of the series to date and then examines how the Greek word 'presbyteros' (elder) is used in the New Testament: out of 67 occurrences, 60 relate to the office rather than mere age, and the first use specifically for Christian leaders appears in Acts 11:30 when the Antioch church sends famine relief to the elders of the Jerusalem church.

The lesson establishes that the three titles—elder, bishop/overseer, and pastor/shepherd—are synonyms referring to the same office with the same persons, describing three different functions: ruling, oversight/keeping the flock together, and leading the flock toward God. The interchangeability is demonstrated from Acts 20:17-28 and 1 Peter 5:1-4, where the same persons are simultaneously called elders, overseers, and shepherds. Notably, 1 Peter 2:25 calls Christ Himself an 'overseer/bishop,' setting the pattern for what elders should be and do.

The plurality of elders in each local congregation is demonstrated positively from Acts 14:23 (elders in every church), Acts 20:17 (the elders of the Ephesian church), Philippians 1:1 (bishops and deacons in the single church of Philippi), and James 5:14 (the earliest New Testament book). This is not merely a Presbyterian conviction: Mark Dever (Southern Baptist), Martin Bucer (continental Reformed), and Iain Murray (Reformed Church of Australia) all affirm the biblical pattern.

Finally, the lesson addresses the modern evangelical attempt to distinguish 'elder-led' from 'elder-ruled' churches, arguing that this is an incoherent attempt to preserve congregationalism while acknowledging the biblical data. The word used in 1 Timothy 5:17, 1 Thessalonians 5:12, 1 Timothy 3:4-5, and Hebrews 13:17 consistently means 'to rule,' not merely 'to lead,' and the Jerusalem Council's decree demonstrates that elders exercise genuine command authority over the churches.
transcript:
## Review of the Series So Far

This is our fourth lesson on the series on church government. The reason why we're doing this is because it is important. The Lord expects something from us as part of His church, and it's of ultimate importance because it says that our life will be better if we obey those who rule over us—that's what the Holy Spirit says in Hebrews 13:17. So in order to be able to do what God wants us to do, it's good for us to know what it is that we're supposed to submit to, what structures the Lord is putting in place for us.

And we've seen that God loves His church. And because of that—not in spite of that—because God loves His church, He has appointed a particular government for her. For the past three Sundays we have been considering that a plurality of biblically qualified elders is God's way to shepherd His flock as prescribed in the scriptures of the Old and New Testament. We are also in the process of seeing that God has appointed deacons to assist the elders in dedicating themselves to the ministry of the Word and prayer.

Today we're going to add two more pieces to our theology of church government. But before we go there, we're going to review some of the stuff that we have already covered in the last three weeks.

We started by looking at the concept of authority and of submission. We saw that authority is God's good creation gift for sharing His rule and glory, and we saw that authority can be Satan's sinister scheme for supplanting God. And following the pattern of the scriptures of creation, fall, and redemption, authority is Christ's claim to rescue and redeem His church as well.

When we talk about authority, we also have to talk about submission, because they are two sides of the same coin. We saw that submission is the path to growth, authority, and likeness to Jesus. We cannot grow to be more like Jesus if we are not practicing submission. Submission is never absolute and always has limits. We also saw that every one of us both practices authority and is under submission at some level.

Then we took a look at how good authority works: that authority is not unaccountable but submits to a higher authority; that authority doesn't steal life but creates it; that authority is not unteachable but seeks wisdom; that authority is neither permissive nor authoritarian but administers discipline; and that authority is not self-protective but bears cost.

We also took a look at authority as a sort of command—those positions of authority that are able to punish if you don't follow—and authority as a sort of council, which is equally authoritative but doesn't have the authority to punish.

Then we took a look at the different models of government that are present in the Church of Jesus Christ: Episcopacy, independent congregationalism, the single pastor model, independent elder-ruled congregationalism (the Puritan model), and the Presbyterian/Reformed model. We took a brief look at the BPC Form of Government. And then the most important thing we've seen so far is that Christ is the only head of the church.

Then last week we took a look at how the terms elder, bishop/overseer, and pastor/shepherd are used in the Old Testament and began examining the New Testament.

## Elder and Related Terms in the New Testament

While 'presbyteros' (elder) finds its origin in Jewish culture, the other term that we've seen applies to elders—'episkopos' or bishop or overseer—comes from the Greek world, but describes a function: the elders are in the church to *oversee* the flock of Christ. The word 'elder' has to do with ruling; 'overseer' has to do with keeping people together, going after the sheep that has gone away; and 'shepherd' has to do with leading and moving the people toward God. These are three functions described by these words.

These two words—'presbyteros' and 'episkopos'—are used interchangeably throughout the New Testament. Although Paul calls the overseer an office in 1 Timothy 3, Peter says that the elder fills the function of oversight. Paul says in Acts 20:28: *'Elders, shepherd the flock for which your overseers'*; Peter says in 1 Peter 5: *'Elders, shepherd the flock for which your overseers.'* They're talking about the same thing.

And there's something important about this word 'episkopos' for us: it is one of the titles given to Christ in the New Testament. In 1 Peter 2:25, Peter says: *For you were like sheep going astray, but have now returned to the Shepherd and Overseer of your souls.* So Jesus is both the Shepherd and the Overseer—the Bishop—of our souls. That gives us an example and pattern to follow. And it's significant that elders who are to rule the church bear the same title as Jesus, the ultimate ruler of the church.

'Presbyteros' is used 67 times in the New Testament. Of those, 60 relate to the office—not to age. The word literally just means someone who is older than somebody else, but it's only used in that sense seven times. The other 60 have to do with those that have rule of some sort. In the Gospels, 'the elders of Israel' always refers to the rulers opposing Jesus. The term is first used favorably as a title for Christian leaders in Acts 11:30—the first time it's used to say, 'These are the leaders of the Church of Jesus Christ'—where the church in Antioch sends famine relief to the elders of the Jerusalem church via Barnabas and Saul.

## The Scriptural Teaching of a Plurality of Elders

So we can see that the Bible gives us a positive model for the government of the church, and that model is a plurality of elders. Any questions or comments before we continue?

Now it's not just an implication—the Scriptures positively teach that each congregation should be shepherded by more than one elder. How many? That's left for the wisdom of God. More than one—at least two.

A significant passage is Acts 14:23. Paul and Barnabas are going on their first missionary journey, ready to establish churches in the region of modern-day Turkey. This is what Luke writes by the inspiration of the Spirit: *When they had appointed elders in every church, they prayed with fasting and commended them to the Lord in whom they had believed.* Now the term 'church' here is used to signify an assembly of Christians regardless of size. And each of these local churches—in Lystra, Derbe, Iconium, and Pisidian Antioch—Paul and Barnabas appointed *what?* Elders—plural—in *every* church—singular. Plural elders going with a singular 'every church.' So you can see here the practice: apostolic, inspired practice for the Church of Jesus Christ. This is important because we're already seeing in the book of Acts a transition from rule by the apostles to rule by elders.

When Paul desired to bid farewell to the leaders of the Ephesian church, he did not call for the single elder of that church. He asked for *the elders* of that particular church to join him. Acts 20:17: *From Miletus he sent to Ephesus and called for the elders of the church.* Not the church in that region, but the church in Ephesus—and the elders of *that* church. Then in verse 28 he addresses them: *Therefore take heed to yourselves and to all the flock among which the Holy Spirit has made you overseers.* In the south we'd say, *Y'all are made overseers*—all these elders together are overseers of this one church.

The single church of Philippi had a plurality of overseers or bishops. Philippians 1:1: *Paul and Timothy, bondservants of Jesus Christ, to all the saints in Christ Jesus who are in Philippi, with the bishops and deacons.* One church of Philippi has a plurality of deacons and a plurality of elders.

A very important passage is James 5:14: *Is anyone among you sick? Let him call for the elders of the church, and let them pray over him, anointing him with oil in the name of the Lord.* Somebody's sick—you call for the elders of your church. You belong to this particular church; this particular church has elders governing it. Not every elder everywhere, not one elder over here, but the elders of *this* church, to whom you belong.

This is even more significant in that James was likely the earliest book written in the New Testament—in the early 40s—which tells us that from the earliest days of the apostolic church, each local church already had a plurality of elders. It wasn't something invented later, but from the very beginning.

This is not only the position of Bible-believing confessional Presbyterian denominations. Southern Baptist minister Mark Dever writes: "The Bible clearly models a plurality of elders in each local church. Though it never suggests a specific number of elders for a particular congregation, the New Testament refers to elders in the plurality in the local churches."

Martin Bucer, a first-generation Reformer who grew up in the Roman Catholic Church, teaches that the Holy Spirit "wishes to have elders in each congregation to exercise the episcopal ministry." This is significant because Bucer is a first-generation reformer, yet this idea—plurality of elders in every local church—was important enough to him that he maintained it.

Iain Murray of the Reformed Church of Australia says: "Each church has elders, also referred to as overseers, just as in the Old Testament each tribe and city had elders." So you can see that even though this is the standard position for Bible-believing confessional Presbyterian denominations, it is something also adopted by others who think the Bible is important.

## Elders Are to Rule the Church

The second new thing we're adding today: elders are to rule the church.

It has become popular in the evangelical world to talk about 'elder-led' churches as if they're something different than 'elder-ruled' churches. The Bible does not talk about elder-led churches as something different than elder-ruled churches. This term is usually used in an attempt to preserve a congregational form of government while at the same time dealing with the overwhelmingly biblical teaching that the church must be shepherded by elders.

The problem with an elder-led model is that it cannot be implemented. How do you implement a model of government where you're supposed to be led by the leaders, but you can choose whether you follow them or not? What happens when the congregation decides not to follow the elders—are the elders then supposed to start ruling? At which point you're not an elder-led church. Or is the congregation not to have the say? At which point you're not a congregational church. Nine Marks Ministries is a big proponent of the elder-led ministry, but no local church actually practices it.

But the Bible teaches that elders are to rule. In 1 Timothy 5:17, Paul says: *Let the elders who rule well be counted worthy of double honor, especially those who labor in the word and doctrine.* Who rule *well*—not just lead, not just be an example, but *rule.* And to rule means to rule.

This word ('prohistemi') is used eight times in the New Testament, all by the Apostle Paul. Romans 12:8: *He who leads, with diligence*—same word as 'rule' in 1 Timothy 5:17. 1 Thessalonians 5:12: *We urge you, brethren, to recognize those who labor among you and are over you in the Lord*—'are over you' is the same word as 'rule' in 1 Timothy 5:17.

In 1 Timothy 3:4-5, talking about qualifications for elders, Paul says: *One who rules his own house well, having his children in submission with all reverence; for if a man does not know how to rule his own house, how will he take care of the church of God?* Is that how we see the role of father in the family—that the children get to pick whether they're going to follow what the dad says or not? I hope that's not how you're doing things in your home.

And in Hebrews 13:17: *Obey those who rule over you and be submissive, for they watch out for your souls as those who must give an account. Let them do so with joy and not with grief, for that would be unprofitable for you.* Obey—not 'decide every time whether you should be led by them or not.' Obey. Our attitude should be first to follow, not to just resist. And then if we cannot obey because it's against the Scriptures, it should be a matter of grief.

I think we have developed as an American church the attitude that we bought into the bumper sticker: "Question Authority." As if there's a virtue in that. Who printed that bumper sticker first? Satan. That's what Satan told Eve: question God's authority.

We also see that the elders gathered for the Jerusalem Council expected all the churches to obey their conclusion. They didn't say, "Hey, here are some suggestions, maybe you can implement them or not." No, they expected obedience. Acts 15:28-29: *For it seemed good to the Holy Spirit and to us to lay upon you no greater burden than these necessary things: that you abstain from things offered to idols, from blood, from things strangled, and from sexual immorality.* They are expecting the church to obey what they are saying.

And these passages indicate that the elders of the church have an authority of command over those entrusted to them. Members of the church are required to obey the elders of the church.

The BPC Form of Government, chapter 8, paragraph 6, says: *The church session is charged with maintaining the spiritual government of the congregation, which purpose they have power to inquire into the knowledge and Christian conduct of the members of the church; to call before them offenders and witnesses; to receive members into the church... to suspend, to exclude from the sacraments those who are found to deserve censure; to determine all matters concerning the conduct of worship and other religious services; to conserve the best measures for promoting the spiritual interests of the congregation, including the supervision of the Sunday school and of other organizations within the church.*

Notice that the session is charged with overseeing *all* activities of the church, including other organizations within the church. This makes sense: the elders should be aware of and have a say in everything that goes on in the local church.

## What Comes Next

So we've seen two new things today. First, the terms elder, bishop, and pastor refer to the same office and are used interchangeably. Second, elders are to rule in the church.

Next week, we're going to start looking at what qualifies a man to serve as an elder or as a deacon in the Church of Jesus Christ.

Let's pray together. Gracious God, we thank You that You are a God who appointed a government for Your church and made it clear in Your word. Father, we pray that You would help us to be faithful to You as a congregation, as a denomination. We pray that we would be people who will follow Your Scriptures as closely as we can by the power of Your Spirit. In Jesus' name. Amen.
